card.title: How a Raindrop Is Actually Born
card.teaser: Before it falls on your window, a raindrop needs a piece of dust, a crowd of friends, and a very patient cloud.
card.coverImagePrompt: An extreme close-up of a single fat raindrop mid-fall, backlit against a blurred gray storm sky, tiny dust mote suspended at the drop's center, photographic, cinematic

lessons.title: Pure water won't rain
lessons.body: Here's something strange: if the air only held clean, pure water vapor, rain would almost never fall. Water molecules don't enjoy sticking together out of thin air — they need something to grab onto first. Every raindrop you've ever seen started its life wrapped around a piece of dirt.

visual.imagePrompt: A microscopic view of water molecules hovering in air, struggling to come together, stylized scientific visualization

lessons.title: The seed of a drop
lessons.body: Clouds form around tiny floating particles called cloud condensation nuclei — specks of dust, sea salt, soot, pollen, even bacteria. Water vapor condenses on these specks, building a droplet only a hundredth of a millimeter across. A single cloud holds billions of them, and that's the fog you see.

keyTerms.definition: Tiny airborne particles that water vapor can condense onto
A microscopic water droplet around 0.01 mm wide — too small to fall as rain

visual.imagePrompt: A stylized diagram showing a salt crystal in the air with water molecules layering around it to form a droplet, soft blue palette

lessons.title: From droplet to drop
lessons.body: A cloud droplet is far too light to fall — it would evaporate before reaching the ground. To become rain, roughly a million droplets have to merge into one. They do this by colliding as they drift and jostle inside the cloud, or by growing on ice crystals that attract water from nearby droplets. Only once a drop reaches about 0.5 mm across does gravity win.

keyTerms.definition: The process where small cloud droplets collide and merge into larger ones

visual.imagePrompt: A slow-motion animation-style image of cloud droplets colliding and merging into a single larger drop inside a cloud

lessons.title: Why it's not a teardrop
lessons.body: Cartoon rain is pointy. Real rain isn't. A falling raindrop is squashed by air resistance into the shape of a tiny hamburger bun — flat on the bottom, rounded on top. Very large drops even split in half as they fall. High-speed camera studies by NASA atmospheric physicists have captured the exact moment a 5 mm drop breaks apart in mid-air.
lessons.funFact: The biggest raindrops ever measured, over Brazil and the Marshall Islands, were nearly 1 cm across — the size of a small grape.

visual.imagePrompt: High-speed photograph of a real raindrop mid-fall, clearly flattened like a hamburger bun, black background, studio lighting

lessons.title: Every storm is also a dust storm
lessons.body: Next time it pours, remember: the clean sheet of water running down your window is actually made of a million tiny collisions, each one built on a speck of desert dust, ocean salt, or factory smoke. Rain isn't just water returning to Earth — it's the sky doing its own laundry.

quiz.question: What does a water droplet need to form inside a cloud?
options: A tiny particle to condense around
quiz.explanation: Water vapor condenses onto condensation nuclei — tiny airborne particles like dust, salt, or soot.

quiz.question: Roughly how many cloud droplets merge to form a single raindrop?
quiz.explanation: It takes on the order of a million microscopic cloud droplets to build one falling raindrop.

quiz.question: What shape is a real falling raindrop?
options: A teardrop with a point on top
quiz.explanation: Air resistance squashes falling drops into a rounded top with a flat base — nothing like the cartoon teardrop.

sources.title: The Physics of Clouds and Precipitation
